title: Text Back vs Snap Back
hook: Still waiting for them to text back. Meanwhile, this whole game took ten minutes to set up.

body.caption: No shade to anyone currently refreshing their messages — we've all been there. We just think there's a faster way to find out if you like someone: play a game with them this week instead of waiting for three dots to turn into a sentence. KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.

title: The Only Personality Test That Actually Works
hook: You can't tell someone's personality from a bio. You can tell everything from how they react to a bad shot.

body.caption: You can't tell someone's personality from six photos and a two-line bio. You can tell everything from how they react to a shot that goes straight into the net, or wide, or nowhere close. Do they laugh? Blame the wind? Ask for a rematch on the spot? Now you know more than three weeks of messaging would ever tell you. That's the whole idea behind KeepItUp — skip the small talk, go straight to the small game. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: REVISION answering owner comment ('use company like branding, put logo and text like a poster'). Full poster treatment: KeepItUp mark top-right, headline block lower third ('THE ONLY PERSONALITY TEST THAT ACTUALLY WORKS') off-white on anthracite scrim with neon-green underline, subhead beneath ('a bio can't show you this. a bad shot can.'), footer band with wordmark. Base photo (NEW): pexels-8795115-badminton-sunset.jpg — two adults playing badminton at golden hour (swapped off the padel photo: it carries a third-party club logo, off-brand for a company poster). (Composite to be baked: b9p02-personality-test.png)

title: The Game Doesn't End When The Game Ends
hook: Most apps stop caring the second you close them. KeepItUp keeps one thing going after the game ends.

body.caption: Most apps stop caring the second you close them. KeepItUp keeps one thing going after the game ends: quick feedback on how it went, and a private note to yourself — nobody else sees it. Over time that becomes your own 'Movement Arc,' a running story of the games you've played. Not a leaderboard. Just your own record of showing up. KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: REVISION answering owner comment ('improve the image and use it with brand logo like a poster'). Full poster treatment: KeepItUp mark bottom-right, headline block upper-middle third ('THE GAME DOESN'T END WHEN THE GAME ENDS') off-white on anthracite scrim, subhead ('your own Movement Arc — private, just for you'), footer band with wordmark. Base photo (NEW): pexels-5807872-cyclists-roadside-chat.jpg — three cyclists pausing to chat on a forest road, a natural 'after' moment. (Composite to be baked: b9p03-game-doesnt-end.png)

title: You Don't Have To Be Looking For Love To Show Up
hook: Dating, friendship, or just a group to play with — you pick, and none of the three is the 'real' reason.

body.caption: When you join KeepItUp, we ask you one honest question: why are you here? Dating, making friends, or just wanting a group to play with — you pick, it shows on your profile, and you can change it whenever you want. Nobody has to guess your reason for showing up, and none of the three is the 'real' one. All of that is exactly what KeepItUp is for. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: REVISION answering owner comment ('use the image like a poster to send the message with brand logo so on'). Full poster treatment: KeepItUp mark top-left, headline block lower third ('YOU DON'T HAVE TO BE LOOKING FOR LOVE TO SHOW UP') off-white on anthracite scrim, subhead ('dating. friendship. or just a group to play with — you pick.'), footer band with wordmark. title: A Short History Of Finding Someone
trend: Replaces the denied 'Guess Which Era' post. Owner: make a simple, easy-to-understand post about the first online dating service in history vs. how Gen Z meets people now.
hook: Online dating didn't start with an app. It started with a computer, a paper questionnaire, and 1965.

slides: A SHORT HISTORY OF FINDING SOMEONE
1965: A COMPUTER MATCHED STRANGERS FROM A PAPER QUESTIONNAIRE. IT WAS THE MATCH.COM OF ITS DAY. (An even earlier version ran in the UK a year before, in 1964.)
TODAY: MOST GEN Z DATERS SAY THE APPS BURN THEM OUT. MANY ARE SHOWING UP TO RUN CLUBS AND REAL GAMES INSTEAD.
SAME IDEA. EASIER VERSION. KEEPITUP — OPEN WORLDWIDE, EARLY ACCESS, LINK IN BIO.
body.caption: Online dating didn't start with an app. In 1965, Harvard students built a computer questionnaire that matched over a million strangers on paper printouts — a similar service had even launched in the UK a year earlier. Skip to today: most Gen Z daters say the apps burn them out, and a lot of them are meeting people at run clubs and real-life groups instead. Same idea, both times — find people through something real. KeepItUp just makes the 'something real' part easy: a small local game or run, near you. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: 4-slide carousel, poster-branded throughout, KeepItUp mark visible on cover + reveal. Slide 1 (photo, NEW: pexels-5384620-basketball-friends.jpg, multiracial friends playing basketball) as cover with eyebrow text. Slide 2: original illustrated punch-card/printout icon (no real photo — no rights issue, same approach as the batch-05 marathon-assist post), anthracite background. Slide 3 (photo, reused from P4: pexels-31293814-park-fitness-group.jpg) for the 'today' beat. Slide 4: reveal card, anthracite, KeepItUp wordmark centered. Sources verified 2026-07-06: Computer History Museum, CNN (2024-09-29), NPR (2025-12-26) on Operation Match (1965) and Joan Ball's St James Computer Dating Service (UK, 1964); Forbes Gen Z dating-app-burnout survey coverage, Time and AOL reporting on Gen Z run-club/real-life-group trend. (Composites to be baked: b9p05-history-{1..4}.png)

title: The 5 Stages Of 'We Should Hang Out Sometime'
hook: A true story that never happened. The 5 stages of 'we should hang out sometime' — starting at step 1.

slides: A TRUE STORY THAT NEVER HAPPENED: THE 5 STAGES OF 'WE SHOULD HANG OUT SOMETIME.'
STEP 1: THE PLAN IS BORN. ENERGY: IMMACULATE. VOICE MEMO LENGTH: 47 SECONDS.
STEP 2: 'THIS WEEK IS CRAZY, WHAT ABOUT NEXT WEEK?' NEXT WEEK HAS ENTERED THE CHAT.
STEP 3: THE THREAD GOES QUIET. NOT A FIGHT. JUST... GRAVITY.
STEP 4: SOMEONE POSTS A STORY FROM SOMEWHERE FUN. THE OTHER PERSON LIKES IT. THAT'S THE WHOLE INTERACTION NOW.
STEP 5: SIX MONTHS LATER, 'WE SHOULD REALLY CATCH UP' — THE SEQUEL NOBODY ASKED FOR.
OR: SKIP ALL 5 STEPS. THERE'S A SMALL LOCAL GAME HAPPENING TONIGHT, NEAR YOU. KEEPITUP — OPEN WORLDWIDE, EARLY ACCESS, LINK IN BIO.
body.caption: This happens about 4 million times a week and has never once made it past step 5. We got tired of watching a good plan fade out one 'next week' at a time, so we skip straight to the part where there's just a game tonight, no planning required. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
body.cta: Comment which step your longest 'we should hang out' thread is stuck on.
body.imageConcept: REVISION answering owner comment ('change steps order, start with step 1') — reordered from a countdown (5→0) to a count-up (Step 1→5) so it reads correctly on a forward-swiping carousel, ending on a reveal that breaks the pattern. Slides 2-6 reuse the existing repo photo pexels-5319397-three-adults-jog.jpg (moody/muted) as a deliberately fading-energy background with bold neon-green step numerals; slide 7 (payoff) switches to a brand-NEW photo, pexels-8795115-badminton-sunset.jpg (warm, golden hour) — full poster branding (KeepItUp mark + wordmark) on cover and reveal slides. (Composites to be baked: b9p06-5-stages-{1..7}.png)

title: The Goalkeeper Whose Life Changed
trend: World Cup 2026 human-interest theme, verified but anonymized — see compliance note below.
hook: Some people's whole life changes the moment someone finally sees them play.

body.caption: This World Cup, a goalkeeper who'd spent years working a regular day job just to keep playing finally got his shot on the world's biggest stage — and made history doing it. By the next morning, millions of people knew his story. (We're not using his name or photo here — it's his story to tell, not ours to use for reach; widely reported by ESPN, Forbes, and beIN Sports if you want to look it up.) The part that gets us: none of it happens without someone showing up to play, year after year, long before anyone was watching. That's the whole idea behind KeepItUp too — just smaller, and every week. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: COMPLIANCE NOTE: trend-jacking-playbook.md rule 2 bars using a real person's name, likeness, or photo without rights — the playbook's own worked example is this exact scenario ('a goalkeeper's heroic save goes viral -> some people find their perfect match at a match; no real name/photo'). This post rides the verified 2026 World Cup theme (a keeper's day-job-to-hero story, reported by ESPN/Forbes/beIN Sports) with an ORIGINAL illustrated goalkeeper silhouette (mid-save, plain kit, no crest/colors/number/name) instead of a real photo. Full poster branding: KeepItUp mark + wordmark, eyebrow 'A TRUE STORY FROM THIS WORLD CUP (NAME WITHHELD — NOT OURS TO TELL)', headline block. No base photo — original art only. (Composite to be baked: b9p07-goalkeeper-story.png)

title: The Rowers' High
hook: Oxford rowers who trained together, in sync, had a bigger pain-threshold jump than rowers who trained the exact same routine alone.

body.caption: In 2009, Oxford researchers had rowers train for 45 minutes, once as a synchronized team and once alone. After training together, the rowers' pain thresholds rose more than after training solo — a sign of a bigger endorphin release from moving in sync with other people (Cohen, Ejsmond-Frey, Knight & Dunbar, Biology Letters, 2009). Same effort. More reward, just from doing it together. That's most of what a group run or a five-a-side game gives you for free. KeepItUp helps you find that game near you. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: NEW science post (owner: 'use science to prove our point like this' on the synchrony/trust post — this is a different, new citation, not a repeat). Full poster treatment: KeepItUp mark + wordmark, eyebrow 'THE SCIENCE OF MOVING TOGETHER', headline block ('MOVE IN SYNC WITH OTHERS AND YOUR BODY RELEASES MORE FEEL-GOOD CHEMICALS THAN MOVING ALONE DOES'), small citation subtext. Base photo (reused from P3): pexels-5807872-cyclists-roadside-chat.jpg. Source verified 2026-07-06: Cohen, Ejsmond-Frey, Knight & Dunbar, 'Rowers' high: behavioural synchrony is correlated with elevated pain thresholds,' Biology Letters (Royal Society), 2009. (Composite to be baked: b9p09-rowers-high.png)
